When contact is strongly structured and susceptibility is heterogeneous, we suggest reporting $R_0$ along with its eigenvectors rather than reporting it alone.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that the basic reproduction number R₀, defined as the spectral radius of the next-generation matrix, discards crucial eigenvector information about epidemic social structure. Analyzing 177 national contact matrices, the authors show the operator is never normal; source and burden eigenvectors are misaligned by a median of 26°. They prove misalignment obeys a Kantorovich bound under reciprocal contact and suggest reporting R₀ alongside its eigenvectors.
AI.motivation: To highlight that the standard definition of R₀ as the spectral radius discards important eigenvector information (burden vs. source distributions) that captures the social structure of epidemics, and to demonstrate the practical consequences of this omission for epidemic control.
AI.method: The authors analyze 177 national contact matrices from Prem et al., examining the (non-)normality of the next-generation operator, eigenvector alignment (angle between left and right dominant eigenvectors), and transient amplification under heterogeneous susceptibility and reciprocal vs. non-reciprocal contact patterns.
AI.result: The operator is never normal across all 177 matrices. With age-specific susceptibility, source and burden eigenvectors are misaligned by a median of 26°, exceeding 40° in some countries. Under reciprocal contact, misalignment obeys a Kantorovich bound determined solely by susceptibility contrast q_max/q_min and is zero under uniform susceptibility. Transient amplification remains small.
AI.conclusion: Reporting R₀ alone is insufficient for structured populations with heterogeneous susceptibility; the authors suggest supplementing R₀ with its eigenvectors to capture the mismatch between transmission sources and burden distribution, which has practical implications for intervention targeting.

title: Biological Motifs for Agentic Control
summary:
The transition of Large Language Models (LLMs) from passive generators to autonomous agents has introduced significant challenges in reliability, security, and state management. Current agentic architectures are often constructed ad-hoc, prone to hallucination cascades, infinite loops, and prompt injection attacks. This paper argues that many of these failure modes can be analyzed using control motifs long studied in systems biology, provided the comparison is made at the level of typed interfaces and coordination structure rather than literal biological mechanism.
  We develop a typed interface correspondence between Gene Regulatory Networks and agentic software systems using polynomial functors and wiring diagrams. Five biological motifs are mapped to composable software design patterns: Coherent Feed-Forward Loops for noise suppression, Adaptive Immunity for layered security, Mitochondrial Signaling for resource governance, Endosymbiosis for neuro-symbolic integration, and Morphogen Diffusion for spatially varying coordination. An epistemic topology layer derives Kripke-style knowledge operators from the wiring diagram's observation structure and proves four predictive theorems for multi-agent scaling.
  The core contributions are: (1) the Agentic Operad, a typed syntax for agent composition with provable error suppression bounds for feed-forward topologies; (2) an epistemic topology with four theorems (error amplification, sequential penalty, parallel acceleration, and tool density scaling) whose qualitative predictions are consistent with published multi-agent benchmarks; and (3) a six-layer progression from structure through development, grounded in autonomous learning frameworks and convergence proxies from the empirical literature. A reference implementation with 1,813 tests and 116 examples illustrates practical feasibility.

AI.tldr: This paper maps biological control motifs (Gene Regulatory Networks) to agentic LLM architectures using polynomial functors and wiring diagrams. It introduces the Agentic Operad (a typed composition syntax), an epistemic topology with four theorems for multi-agent scaling, and a six-layer development progression. Validated by 1,813 tests and 116 examples.
AI.motivation: LLMs transitioning to autonomous agents suffer from hallucination cascades, infinite loops, and prompt injection attacks. Current architectures are ad-hoc and unreliable. The authors argue these failure modes can be analyzed through control motifs from systems biology when compared at the level of typed interfaces and coordination structure.
AI.method: Typed interface correspondence between Gene Regulatory Networks and agentic systems using polynomial functors and wiring diagrams. Five biological motifs mapped to software design patterns. Epistemic topology layer deriving Kripke-style knowledge operators from observation structures. The Agentic Operad provides typed syntax with provable error suppression bounds.
AI.result: Three core contributions: (1) Agentic Operad with provable error suppression bounds for feed-forward topologies; (2) epistemic topology with four theorems (error amplification, sequential penalty, parallel acceleration, tool density scaling) consistent with published multi-agent benchmarks; (3) six-layer progression from structure through development. 1,813 tests and 116 examples demonstrate feasibility.
AI.conclusion: Biological control motifs provide a principled foundation for designing reliable, secure, and composable multi-agent LLM systems. The formal framework (Agentic Operad + epistemic topology) offers provable guarantees and predictive theorems that align with empirical multi-agent benchmarks.

title: Causal ASCEND: Scalable Two-tier Causal Discovery on High Dimensional Multi-omics Data
comment: Main material: 8 pages + supplementary material. 16 pages in all
summary: Biological systems exhibit a hierarchical structure, characterised by directed flow from upstream regulators to downstream effects. Although this ordering provides a natural scaffold for causal inference, most causal discovery and GRN methods either ignore the tiered organisation or condition on all upstream variables, which becomes infeasible for high-dimensional omics data. We present ASCEND (Ancestral Scalable Causal discovEry via iNherited Descent), a constraint-based framework that leverages known two-tiered structure to enable genome-scale causal discovery. ASCEND introduces a divide-and-conquer strategy that maintains dynamically updated ancestral conditioning sets for each downstream variable, dramatically reducing the number of conditional independence tests required, and achieves polynomial-time complexity where traditional approaches face exponential blow-up. Through extensive simulations and real biological data, we demonstrate that ASCEND accurately recovers ancestral relationships, scales properly and much faster, and outperforms existing gene regulatory network inference methods in both causal precision and computational efficiency. The algorithm's ability to resolve directionality makes it particularly suited for integrating multi-omic data where upstream regulators (e.g., SNPs, methylation sites) and downstream responses (e.g., gene expression) are measured jointly.

AI.tldr: ASCEND is a scalable causal discovery framework that leverages known two-tiered biological structure (upstream→downstream) with a divide-and-conquer strategy to achieve polynomial-time causal inference for genome-scale data, outperforming existing methods in accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Biological systems have hierarchical structure with directed flow, but existing causal discovery and GRN methods either ignore this tiered organization or condition on all upstream variables (infeasible for high-dimensional omics data).
AI.method: ASCEND introduces a constraint-based framework with a divide-and-conquer strategy using dynamically updated ancestral conditioning sets for each downstream variable, dramatically reducing conditional independence tests and achieving polynomial-time complexity.
AI.result: ASCEND accurately recovers ancestral relationships, scales properly with much faster speed, and outperforms existing GRN inference methods in both causal precision and computational efficiency in simulations and real biological data.
AI.conclusion: ASCEND enables genome-scale causal discovery by leveraging tiered structure, resolving directionality, and is particularly suited for integrating multi-omic data (e.g., SNPs, methylation sites → gene expression).

title: Predicting Therapeutic Outcome via Aligning Patient-Specific Knowledge Graph and Gene-Level Perturbation Representations
comment: 12 pages, 5 figures, 5 tables. Accepted at BIOKDD 2026, held in conjunction with ACM SIGKDD 2026
summary: Accurate prediction of patient-specific therapeutic response from pre-treatment transcriptomes is hindered by the scarcity of matched clinical response labels and post-treatment molecular profiles. Preclinical transfer-learning models can simulate drug-induced expression changes but are often hard to interpret and unstable, whereas knowledge-graph methods provide mechanistic context yet remain static and fail to capture drug-induced transcriptomic perturbation dynamics. We propose PREDIKTOR, a patient-centered multi-view framework that aligns a personalized network view with a transferable transcriptomic perturbation view to predict clinical drug response. For each patient, we construct an individualized gene regulatory network from tumor expression using DysRegNet and augment it with drug-target links from DrugBank; a graph neural encoder yields a drug-centric, mechanistically grounded embedding. In parallel, a frozen condition-specific gene-gene attention model pretrained on LINCS L1000 generates a simulated post-perturbation transcriptomic profile for the same patient-drug pair. We align the two views in a shared latent space via a CLIP-style contrastive objective with drug-context hard negatives, then concatenate the representations for end-to-end response classification. On TCGA, PREDIKTOR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines under patient-, drug-, and tissue-split evaluations, and transfers zero-shot to the I-SPY2 trial, improving AUROC by 5.6% over competing methods. The aligned embeddings yield stable gene and pathway attributions that recover known mechanisms, supporting actionable and interpretable precision oncology.

AI.tldr: PREDIKTOR is a multi-view framework that combines personalized gene regulatory networks (via DysRegNet + DrugBank) with simulated drug-induced transcriptomic perturbations (via LINCS L1000 attention model) using contrastive alignment, achieving state-of-the-art clinical drug response prediction on TCGA and zero-shot transfer to I-SPY2 trials with interpretable mechanism recovery.
AI.motivation: Existing methods fall short: preclinical transfer-learning models are unstable and hard to interpret, while knowledge-graph methods are static and cannot capture drug-induced transcriptomic perturbation dynamics. There is a need for an interpretable, stable, and accurate approach to predict patient-specific therapeutic responses from pre-treatment transcriptomes.
AI.method: PREDIKTOR uses two views per patient-drug pair: (1) a personalized gene regulatory network (via DysRegNet + drug-target links from DrugBank) encoded by a graph neural network, and (2) a simulated post-perturbation transcriptomic profile from a frozen LINCS L1000-pretrained attention model. These views are aligned via CLIP-style contrastive learning with hard negatives, then concatenated for end-to-end response classification.
AI.result: PREDIKTOR outperforms baselines on TCGA under patient-, drug-, and tissue-split evaluations, and transfers zero-shot to the I-SPY2 trial with a 5.6% AUROC improvement. The aligned embeddings produce stable gene and pathway attributions that recover known mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: PREDIKTOR provides an interpretable, stable, and accurate solution for clinical drug response prediction by integrating personalized network context with perturbation dynamics, supporting actionable precision oncology.

title: Score Distributions, Not Cells: Evaluating Single-Cell Perturbations Under Class Overlap
summary:
Most classification problems assume the classes are roughly separable, so that an individual sample can usually be assigned to one class. Single-cell perturbation data violates this assumption: two perturbations can produce different populations of cells while overlapping so much that an individual cell could belong to either. Per-cell accuracy then measures this overlap rather than model quality. We see this on Tahoe-100M and the Virtual Cell Challenge, where a linear classifier, an MLP, and a Transformer all plateau near macro-F1 0.2-0.3 even though almost every pair of perturbations is statistically distinguishable.
  The fix is to score perturbations across the whole population rather than cell by cell. We average a classifier's per-cell probability vectors over all cells of a perturbation to form a population profile, then rank candidate perturbations by this profile; we call the resulting score the Classifier Discrimination Score (CDS). Taking the top-ranked class recovers the winning perturbation. It needs no retraining, costs linear time in the number of cells, and recovers near-perfect identification from the same weak models. CDS differs from the pseudobulk-based Perturbation Discrimination Score (PDS) used in recent benchmarks only in where the average is taken, raw gene expression for PDS versus a learned discriminative space for CDS, and identifies the true perturbation more reliably on both datasets, with the gap widening as cells grow scarce. Because a metric that misranks the ground truth will misrank the models scored against it, per-cell accuracy and raw-pseudobulk scores should be used with caution when comparing perturbation models.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies that per-cell accuracy is misleading for single-cell perturbation data due to high class overlap, and proposes the Classifier Discrimination Score (CDS)—averaging probability vectors per perturbation—which recovers near-perfect identification from weak models without retraining.
AI.motivation: Standard classification metrics fail on single-cell perturbation data because perturbations produce overlapping cell populations, making per-cell accuracy measure overlap rather than model quality.
AI.method: Average a classifier's per-cell probability vectors over all cells of a perturbation to form a population profile, then rank candidates by this profile (CDS). No retraining needed, linear time cost.
AI.result: CDS recovers near-perfect identification from weak models (linear classifiers plateauing at macro-F1 0.2-0.3). Outperforms per-cell accuracy and PDS on Tahoe-100M and Virtual Cell Challenge, especially with scarce cells.
AI.conclusion: Per-cell accuracy and raw-pseudobulk scores can misrank perturbation models; CDS provides a more reliable alternative for comparing perturbation models.

title: Disguised complex balance via positive algebraic geometry
summary:
We study dynamical systems arising from reaction networks under mass-action kinetics. For certain choices of the rate constants (parameters), such systems are complex-balanced (vertex-balanced), which guarantees the existence of a unique positive equilibrium. Moreover, this equilibrium is asymptotically stable (admitting a global Lyapunov function) and linearly stable.
  In a series of recent papers, Craciun and collaborators introduced and studied disguised complex-balanced systems, that is, mass-action systems that are dynamically equal to auxiliary complex-balanced systems and therefore inherit their strong stability properties. Determining the parameter values for which a given system is disguised complex-balanced is a nontrivial algebraic problem.
  In this work, we show that the defining conditions for disguised complex-balanced equilibria naturally give rise to parametrized systems of polynomial inequalities. Using the framework for positive algebraic geometry developed by Müller and Regensburger, we reformulate these systems as binomial equations (on the disguised complex-balanced flux cone). Computing the disguised complex-balanced parameter locus can be viewed as a quantifier-elimination problem, and our approach eliminates the concentrations (state variables) from the problem.
  We illustrate our results using the running example of a recent paper by Boros et al.

AI.tldr: This paper reformulates disguised complex-balanced mass-action systems as polynomial inequalities and binomial equations, eliminating state variables to compute parameter loci via positive algebraic geometry.
AI.motivation: Disguised complex-balanced systems inherit strong stability properties from auxiliary complex-balanced systems, but determining the parameter values for which a given system is disguised complex-balanced is a nontrivial algebraic problem.
AI.method: The authors show that defining conditions for disguised complex-balanced equilibria yield parametrized polynomial inequalities. Using positive algebraic geometry (Müller & Regensburger), they reformulate these as binomial equations on the disguised complex-balanced flux cone, treating the parameter locus computation as a quantifier-elimination problem that eliminates concentrations.
AI.result: The approach transforms the algebraic problem into binomial equations, enabling elimination of state variables. Results are illustrated using a running example from Boros et al.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a systematic algebraic geometry approach to identify disguised complex-balanced parameter regimes, simplifying stability analysis for mass-action reaction networks.

title: Data-Driven Soft Labeling Scales DNA Read Classification to Whole-Body Cell-Type Deconvolution
summary: Cell-type deconvolution, the task of estimating the proportions of constituent cell types in a heterogeneous biological sample, is a core problem in computational biology. Methods that rely on epigenetic marks such as DNA methylation typically operate on aggregated methylation estimates, discarding the pattern-level information carried by individual DNA reads. Existing read-level approaches that exploit this information are scarce, and all remain restricted to few-class settings; scaling them further is an open problem because, at scale, non-discriminative reads dominate and hard labels conflict with the many-to-many mapping between methylation patterns and cell types, preventing classifier convergence. To overcome this, we propose data-driven soft labels that estimate the conditional cell-type distribution for each read, and integrate this scheme into Syto, a new modular framework for read-level classification-based deconvolution. On a whole-body atlas of 39 human cell types, Syto reduces MSE by 2.56$\times$ over SoTA, with gains transferring to an out-of-distribution dataset spanning 16 tissues. Syto lays the foundation for modeling increasingly large cell-type panels, with improved applications in biology and healthcare. The proposed soft-labeling scheme is further translatable to any setting with a many-to-many signal-to-label mapping.

AI.tldr: Syto is a new modular framework for read-level cell-type deconvolution that uses data-driven soft labels to handle many-to-many mapping between methylation patterns and cell types, reducing MSE by 2.56× over state-of-the-art on a 39-cell-type atlas.
AI.motivation: Existing read-level deconvolution methods are restricted to few-class settings because non-discriminative reads dominate at scale and hard labels conflict with the many-to-many mapping between methylation patterns and cell types, preventing classifier convergence.
AI.method: Proposes data-driven soft labels that estimate the conditional cell-type distribution for each read, integrated into Syto, a modular framework for read-level classification-based deconvolution.
AI.result: On a whole-body atlas of 39 human cell types, Syto reduces MSE by 2.56× over state-of-the-art, with gains transferring to an out-of-distribution dataset spanning 16 tissues.
AI.conclusion: Syto lays the foundation for modeling increasingly large cell-type panels, with improved applications in biology and healthcare. The soft-labeling scheme is translatable to any setting with a many-to-many signal-to-label mapping.

title: Geometric Causal Models
summary: Scientists often seek to draw causal inferences from structured data that is not independently and identically distributed, such as spatial data, network data, or molecular data. We develop geometric causal models (GCMs), a framework for causal inference from dependent data that exploits underlying symmetries of the data generating process. For example, in spatial data, we consider processes that are symmetric under translations, or in graph data, symmetric under permutations of the nodes. We show how symmetries, formalized with group theory, can enable causal identification and estimation. We deploy ergodic theory for amenable groups to establish identification, and combine geometric deep learning with scalable Bayesian inference for estimation. We recover i.i.d. causal models and do-calculus when the data is a sequence and the symmetry is permutation equivariance, and find novel types of causal models when we use alternate structures and symmetries. As an example, we construct a causal model that satisfies the symmetries of DNA. This GCM enables new estimators for the effects of genetic variation, combining deep functional genomics models to describe outcomes and DNA language models to describe propensities. We illustrate on semisynthetic data.

AI.tldr: The paper develops Geometric Causal Models (GCMs), a framework that exploits group-theoretic symmetries (e.g., translation in space, permutation in graphs) to enable causal inference from dependent data, combining ergodic theory for identification and geometric deep learning with Bayesian inference for estimation.
AI.motivation: Scientists need causal inference from non-i.i.d. structured data (spatial, network, molecular), but standard causal methods assume independence. The authors aim to leverage underlying data symmetries to overcome this limitation.
AI.method: The authors formalize symmetries using group theory, use ergodic theory for amenable groups to establish causal identification, and combine geometric deep learning with scalable Bayesian inference for estimation. They extend i.i.d. causal models and do-calculus as special cases under permutation equivariance.
AI.result: GCMs recover i.i.d. causal models and do-calculus for sequence data with permutation symmetry, and discover novel causal models under alternative structures/symmetries. A DNA-specific GCM enables new estimators for genetic variation effects by combining deep functional genomics and DNA language models.
AI.conclusion: Geometric Causal Models provide a principled framework for causal inference on dependent structured data by exploiting symmetries, with demonstrated applicability to genetics and potential for spatial and network data.
